topic: Someone finds an unsigned birthday card in their mailbox
raw_output:
[story 1]

The late afternoon sun cast long, distorted shadows across the porch as I retrieved the mail. It was a routine chore, something I performed with practiced indifference, until my fingers brushed against a thick, cream-colored card tucked between a utility bill and a flyer for a local bake sale. It was completely blank, save for a single, elegantly scripted line at the bottom: "Happy Birthday." My breath hitched. I stared at the paper, the ink seeming to vibrate under my gaze. Who had done this? There was no return address, no signature, nothing to anchor it to reality. I turned the card over, expecting a hidden message, a secret inscription, but it was just smooth, untouched paper. A cold prickle traced its way up my arms. I slipped it into my pocket, the crinkle of the paper sounding deafening in the sudden silence of the yard. I spent the next hour pacing, my jaw tight, unable to focus on anything else. Every rustle of leaves sounded like a deliberate movement.

[story 2]

I opened the mailbox with a sigh, expecting the usual assortment of junk mail and circulars. Instead, nestled amongst the glossy advertisements was a card. It was heavy stock, the kind you usually only see in formal invitations, and it was completely unsigned. The message, printed in a delicate, looping script, simply read: "Wishing you a wonderful day." My heart began to thump a frantic rhythm against my ribs. I picked it up, my palms growing damp. I examined the edges, searching for any seam, any sign of tampering. There was nothing. It felt strangely warm to the touch, almost alive. I walked back inside, placing the card on the kitchen counter. I couldn't bring myself to look at it again for nearly an hour. My focus kept drifting to the front door, listening intently for any sound that might indicate someone was watching, someone waiting just beyond the threshold.

[story 3]

It was the sheer absurdity of the find that first registered—a birthday card, completely unaddressed, deposited in my box. I retrieved it, my movements jerky, almost clumsy. The card was pristine, the paper thick, and the sentiment was as simple as "Happy Birthday." But the lack of personalization was what truly unsettled me. It felt like an intrusion, a deliberate, silent message slipped into my ordinary day. I held it under the harsh light of the hallway fixture, scrutinizing the texture of the paper, searching for any anomaly, any hidden watermark. My throat tightened as I realized I had no idea who it was meant for, or why it had been left there. I tucked it deep into my jeans pocket, the fabric digging slightly into my thigh, and kept glancing towards the window, feeling intensely exposed.
